coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of dual carbon dots using only an N and S co-existed dopant for fluorescence detection of Ag+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Zhenxing
Wang, Wenhai
Zhan, Xiaozhi
Du, Xiujuan
Zhang, Qingmei
Zhang, Rui
Li, Kun
Li, Jinhong
Xu, Weiping
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent carbon-based nanoparticles, named often as carbon dots (CDs), were synthesized from citric acid (CA) and guanidine thiocyanate (GITC) via an N and S co-doped hydrothermal procedure. In the present structure characterization, N and S elements could be sufficiently doped by means of the heteroatom or the functional groups bonded on the surface of CDs. The as-prepared CDs solution showed blue color fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation, yet the PL spectra exhibited a repetitive emission process from excitation-independent to excitation-dependent. In view of the triexponential feature of fluorescence lifetimes of CDs, one possibility was proposed to be co-existence of two types of CDs with different surface states. Additionally, the as-prepared CDs were used as a sensing probe for the detection of Ag+ taking into consideration of the possible interactions between Ag+ and various fluorophores attached to the CD surface. As expected, the changes of fluorescence intensities were linearly proportional to the different concentration ranges of Ag+, which suggests the complex nature of the quenching mechanism. And for the first time, the SCN group was found to accelerate the quenching of CDs towards Ag+, promising a new approach for efficient detection of Ag+ for the application in industrial pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S
N-CDs
Co-existence
Triexponential
Ag+
SCN

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of the S, N-CDs
Optical Properties of the S, N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) HRTEM image of as-prepared N, S-CDs. Inset of (a) is the corresponding size distribution histogram of the N, S-CDs, inset of (b) is a typical single CD with lattice parameter of 0.21 nm.
FTIR spectra of free CA, GITC and CCA+GITC dots. Spectra are offset for clarity.
High-resolution XP spectra of the obtained CCA+GITC dots: (a) Overall picture, (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) S2p.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum (blue line), excitation and emission spectrum (black line) of CCA+GITC dots. Inset: Photograph of CCA+GITC dots under visible light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right).

PL emission spectra of CCA+GITC dots at various excitation wavelengths: (a) 275–385 nm, (b) 390–415 nm, (c) 420–450 nm, (d) 450–520 nm. Gray dotted arrows of (a), (b) and (c) indicates PL emission peak positions are either independent or dependent of excitation wavelength. Inset of (d) is normalized PL emission spectra at excitation wavelengths of 450, 460, 480, 500 and 520 nm respectively.

(a) 3D PL spectra under the excitation wavelengths range from 250 to 450 nm with 5 nm increments and the emission wavelengths range from 425 to 650 nm with 5 nm increments. (b) changes of the emission wavelengths with the excitation wavelengths. Inset: Photograph of CCA+GITC dots under excitation wavelength of 520 nm (right) and schematic diagram of the hydrothermal treatment of CA and GITC (left), which is possible to synthesize two kinds of CDs (labeled as N-CDs and S-CDs) simultaneously, and both have different fluorescence features because one of them becomes not PL with the excitation wavelength up to 420 nm.
Fluorescence decay curves of CCA+GITC dots with different reaction times (a), reaction temperatures (b) and reaction mole ratios (c) recorded at room temperature in aqueous solution.
Comparison of PL intensities of the CCA+GITC dots after adding different metal ions with same concentration of 250 μM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs mixed with various concentrations of Ag+: 0, 1.0, and 10.0 μM, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0, and 10.0 mM from top to bottom. (b) The emission intensity ratio F0/F depends on the concentrations of as-added Ag+, which is derived from the results in (a). F0/F ratios are evaluated by the emission at ac. 470 nm, and the excitation wavelength is set at 395 nm. The Stern-Volmer (red) and lineweaver-Burk fitting (blue in inset) of CDs-Ag+ are performed in front and at the back of the 1 mM concentrations of Ag+ respectively.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) fluorescence decay curves of the CDs as well as that after adding Ag+ (1 mM).
Fitting parameters of the corresponding PL decay curve and QYs of the corresponding CCA+GITC dots.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of dual carbon dots using only an N and S co-existed dopant for fluorescence detection of Ag+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Taiyuan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of High Energy Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, People's Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent carbon-based nanoparticles, named often as carbon dots (CDs), were synthesized from citric acid (CA) and guanidine thiocyanate (GITC) via an N and S co-doped hydrothermal procedure. In the present structure characterization, N and S elements could be sufficiently doped by means of the heteroatom or the functional groups bonded on the surface of CDs. The as-prepared CDs solution showed blue color fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation, yet the PL spectra exhibited a repetitive emission process from excitation-independent to excitation-dependent. In view of the triexponential feature of fluorescence lifetimes of CDs, one possibility was proposed to be co-existence of two types of CDs with different surface states. Additionally, the as-prepared CDs were used as a sensing probe for the detection of Ag+ taking into consideration of the possible interactions between Ag+ and various fluorophores attached to the CD surface. As expected, the changes of fluorescence intensities were linearly proportional to the different concentration ranges of Ag+, which suggests the complex nature of the quenching mechanism. And for the first time, the SCN group was found to accelerate the quenching of CDs towards Ag+, promising a new approach for efficient detection of Ag+ for the application in industrial pollutants.

This figure shows the changes of emission wavelengths with the excitation wavelengths. Inset: Photograph of CCA+GITC dots under excitation wavelength of 520 nm (right) and schematic diagram of the hydrothermal treatment of CA and GITC (left), which is possible to synthesize two kinds of CDs (labeled as N-CDs and S-CDs) simultaneously, and both have different fluorescence features because one of them becomes not PL with the excitation wavelength up to 420 nm.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most striking discoveries in the quest for new nanomaterials, luminescent carbon dots (CDs) have recently drawn increasing attention. Their merits, such as high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, high resistance to photo-bleaching, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [1,2], make them new-generation fluorescent (FL) materials superior to conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [3,4]. Up to now these materials have exhibited great potential applications in the fields of bioimaging [5], photocatalysis [6], LED [7], energy conversion [8], sensing [9], detection of metal ions [10] and medical diagnosis [11], to mention just several. Therefore, various approaches, with a large variety of techniques and starting materials utilized, have been developed to prepare suitable fluorescent CDs. However, as-prepared CDs materials are often low FL properties and haphazard along with the diverse synthesis processes and the types of precursor materials thus far [12], which hinders significantly the development and use of CDs based on luminescent nanomaterials.

The heteroatom doping into CDs is not only an efficient method to enhance their PL properties by tuning their surface/local chemistry and electronic properties [13,14], but is helpful to propelling the theoretical research and provides a direction of practical application. Generally, these doped CDs are prepared by the combination of two types of molecular precursor materials, in which one provides the major carbon framework and another introduces as-doped element(s) into the framework structure. According to this regular, the most prominent combination has been citric acid (CA) as carbon source, while the molecules containing as-doped element(s) (like N [15,16], P [17] and S [18,19]) as the dopants. Among these, N-doping is found to be more facile and effective due to the similar size of N with C atom and the strong valence bonds between N and C atoms [20], which even achieves the N-doping CDs with a fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) as high as 99% from citric acid and tris(hydroxylmethyl)aminomethane by a microwave synthesis [21]. Similarly, S-doping also enhanced the PL properties of as-prepared CDs because S atom could act as a catalytic center for redox reaction to introduce more passivated surface defects in CDs [22]. By far, plenty of works have been focused on the N and S doped CDs (N-CDs and S-CDs) although the corresponding mechanisms remain unclear actually, whereas S and N co-doped CDs (S, N-CDs) has recently been of another great interest. By the interaction between doped atoms it is possible to adjust compositions and structures and improve the PL performance of S, N-CDs. For instance, Dong et al. proposed a simple method for synthesizing S and N co-doped CDs with excitation-independent emission and high QYs, which is mainly resulted from the newly formed surface state related to N atoms, while sulfur atoms plays a cooperative role in view of the PL performance [23]. Ding et al. argued that the PL emission of N, S-CDs was from the same synergetic effects of S and N atoms due to redistribution of spin and charge densities in as-prepared CDs [24]. Zhuo et al. and Yang et al. synthesized other two N, S-CDs respectively and suggested reaction temperature was a key to tune S and N molecular structure, which decided the PL performance of CDs [25]. What's more, Gao et al. suggested that chemical structure motif of dopant containing S and N elements fundamentally determined the optical properties of as-prepared S and N co-doped CDs besides of the rational synthesis condition (time, temperature and molar ratio) [26]. Thus, it is a challenge to further search for the origin of the luminescence enhancement induced by the synergetic effects of S and N atoms in N, S-CDs.

Additionally, as we all know that silver ions (Ag+) has many applications in our daily life and the industries such as electron, photograph and image, while it has been classified as one of the most poisonous heavy metal ions [27,28]. Therefore, fluorescence probes for Ag+ detection for the industrial pollutants have been a hot subject with the discovery of outstanding PL properties of CDs materials recently [29–31]. Unfortunately, fluorescence quenching mechanism of Ag+ has still been an open question due to the flexibility and diversity of prepared CDs up to now. But one thing is for certain, that is luminescence quenching is indeed rooted in the interaction between Ag+ and the functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups) on the surface of CDs [32–34]. Furthermore, it was also reported that luminescence was enhanced upon addition of Ag+ into the CDs solution due to the strong affinity of biothiols towards metal cations and metal nanoparticles [35], which may be ascribed to the metal-enhanced fluorescence mechanism [29,36]. Obviously, CA is selected as carbon source and the S, N atomic dopant includes amino and thiol groups, such a kinds of S, N-CDs prepared would be not only helpful to understand the roles of heteroatom doping (S and N elements) in luminescence enhancement, but is the best candidate to investigate the mechanism of Ag+ detection because it is necessary for the abundant functional groups possible to appear on the surface of CDs to provide all possibility to affect with Ag+.

In the present work, we reported the preparation of S, N-CDs with Guanidine thiocyanate (GITC) as the N and S dopant via a simple hydrothermal synthesis. GITC works as idea raw material because it not only possesses rich N (NH2, NH, N) and S (SH), but the interaction of them is via a hydrogen bond to form the molecule of GITC, which is possible to provide a more flexible pathway to prepare S, N-CDs. Without any doubt, CA is selected as carbon source based on the analyzation above. By optimizing the synthesis conditions, as-prepared CDs solution emitted deep blue color emission, yet the highest QY is only 23%. The relationship of emission spectra with the change of excitation wavelength suggested the PL phenomenon was induced possibly by two types of CDs with different PL properties. The CDs solution is also applied to the fluorescent detection of heavy metal ions without further chemical modifications or surface functionalization and can be quenched obviously with the increasing concentrations of adding Ag+ ions. As expected, it is showed that the rich changes of fluorescence along with the addition of Ag+ is closely associated with SCN group on the surface of CDs through the studies on Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime, thus, which is a promising method for the cost-effective, fast-convenient and scalable fabrication of photoluminescent S, N-CDs when a fluorescent Ag+ nanosensor with high detection selectivity and sensitivity has been built up, and all of the results will be elaborated on in Section 3 in detail.

All the reagents were of at least analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Guanidine thiocyanate (GITC) and Citric acid (CA) monohydrate were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Stock solutions of metal cations were prepared in ultrapure water from the respective salts of AgNO3, MgCl2·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, NaCl, KCl, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, ZnCl2, AlCl3·6H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, BaCl2, LiNO3, which were purchased from several chemical works (Tianjin Guangfu technology development Co. Ltd., Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China), Sino pharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., and so on).

The S and N co-doped CDs were prepared in one pot through a hydrothermal treatment as follows: in a typical preparation procedure, 0.15 g CA monohydrate and 0.7033 g GITC were dissolved in distilled water (15 ml). After vigorously stirring to clear solution at the room temperature, the mixture was poured in Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 2 h. After naturally cooling to the room temperature, the obtained solution containing light yellow suspended particles was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove the larger particles. The final light yellow product was clear and transparent.

With dropping CDs solution onto a super-thin carbon-coated copper grid (200 meshes) to evaporate the excess solvent, transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken with an operating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on an ATR-IR spectrometer (EQUINOX 55, Bruker, Germany) with wavenumber range of 450–4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs was performed using a PHI-5000C ESCA system (PerkinElmer Inc., USA) with Al Kα X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation. UV–visible absorption spectrum was acquired by a UH-4150 UV/Visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the scanning range of 175–3300 nm to determine the bandgap absorption of as-prepared CDs. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs were carried out on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were taken on a steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS-1000, Edinburgh Instruments) to detect the luminescence mechanism. Quantum yields of CDs were measured on a FS5 quantum efficiency measurement system (Edinburgh Instruments). All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions.

To evaluate the selectivity of as-prepared S, N-CDs, aqueous solutions containing eleven kinds of metal ions, Ag+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, K+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, and Li+, were prepared respectively with a concentration of 1 M, and then 1 mL of the above metal ion solution was mixed with 3 mL of the S, N-CDs solution. After 10 minute standing, PL spectra of the mixed solutions were measured for recording the fluorescence intensity. In view of the best quenching to Ag+ in all of the above metal ions, different concentrations of Ag+ (1 mL) were mixed in the standard S, N-CDs solution (4 mL) to test the influence of quenching to them, then the treating S, N-CDs solutions were also incubated for 10 min at the room temperature. Similarly, fluorescence emission spectra of the corresponding supernatants were record using an excitation wavelength at 398 nm for comparison. The control sample was prepared by mixing the same pure water with the standard S, N-CDs solution in the whole process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and Characterization of the S, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since the synthesis of S, N-CDs was easily influenced by the reaction conditions including temperature, time and molar ratio in the processes of thermal treatment, the effect of reaction conditions on the carbonization of CA and GITC mixture was investigated respectively. As shown in Fig. S1a–c, all of the UV–visible absorption spectra exhibit a distinct shoulder peak around 220 nm which is ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [26], yet it is almost no change along with varying reaction condition, suggesting that CA and GITC mixture can easily form the S, N-CDs under the various reaction conditions. The quantum yields of as-prepared CDs at the same reaction conditions, however, show some obvious changes: there is a jump-point appeared at the molar ratio of GITC/CA = 42:5 with increasing gradually molar ratio of GITC/CA as shown in the illustration of Fig. S1a; The similar thing happened in the temperature variations that it turns a corner at 200 °C with growing temperature from 160 to 240 °C at 20 °C intervals in the illustration of Fig. S1b; Unexpectedly, the quantum yields of as-prepared S, N-CDs at the reaction time of 1, 2, 4 and 6 h respectively decline sharply along with increasing reaction times, while it is almost constant reaching 2 h compared to that at 1 h as shown in the illustration of Fig. S1c. Therefore, the aqueous S, N-CDs solution prepared at the molar ratio of GITC/CA = 42:5, 200 °C for 2 h is selected as a typical example to characterize their structure, PL property and fluorescent detection for metal ions, except those specially pointed out.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been extensively utilized as a powerful tool to study the morphology of CDs from which the size can be identified. Fig. 1a displays the TEM image of as-prepared S, N-CDs and shows that the S, N-CDs are nearly spherical, uniform and well dispersed with a size distribution between 3.5 and 7.0 nm (average diameter ~5.0 nm) as shown in the particle size distribution histogram (inset of Fig. 1a). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of prepared S, N-CDs in Fig. 1b exhibits the well-resolved lattice fringes with an average interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which agrees with that of in-plane lattice spacing of graphitic carbon (100 facet) [25,37].

The surface functional groups of the S, N-CDs were analyzed by the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Fig. 2 depicts the FTIR spectra of CA, GITC and as-prepared S, N-CDs labeled as CCA+GITC dots. For the spectrum of CCA+GITC dots, the broad absorption bands at the range of 2750–3500 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH/CH [26,38,39]. Sequentially, a strong peak of 2060 cm−1 can be observed clearly without bordering on the any other bands, which is arranged firstly to the vibration related to CN group taking into consideration of the vibrational property of GITC molecule. Compared with the sharp absorption peak of GITC at 2066 cm−1, CN vibration peak of CCA+GITC dots is rather stable, which is reasonable in view of the chemical inertness of CN triple bond. Unfortunately, SH stretching vibration, existing on the wavenumber of around 2500 cm−1 for a great number of S(N)-doping CDs [24,26], is not found, which suggests that HSCN group is fully doped via the breaking of HS bond. Moreover, the typical absorption peaks of CO/CN stretching (1673/1617 cm−1), amido CON-H bending (1568 cm−1), CN (1456 cm−1) and CS (1361 cm−1) stretching can also be identified by the spectrum of CCA+GITC, providing rich evidences to form the cores as polyaromatic structures, especially some weak peaks at the wavenumber of around 1000 cm−1 which are corresponded to the vibrations of CNC/CSC in Ref. [26]. Obviously, the FTIR spectroscopy testifies that S and N elements are efficiently doped into carbon framework of the CCA+GITC dots according to analysis of the results above.

The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) is employed to investigate the elemental composition and surface states of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 3a, the wide spectrum of S, N-CDs shows three dominant peaks of O1s (532 eV), N1s (400 eV) and C1s (285 eV) and two weak peaks assigned to S1s (227 eV) and S2p (163 eV), respectively [40]. The existence of an S2p peak at 163 eV and the absence of a peak at approximately 170 eV indicate that the doped S atoms exist as the sulfide rather than sulfate/sulfite form in the CDs [41]. With corrected for element sensitivity, the C:N:O:S ratio based on XPS peak was 41.29:32.62:18.91:7.18 for CCA+GITC dots, which declares the incorporation of S and N atoms to the CDs in this work. The high resolution scan of C1s region (Fig. 3b) can be deconvoluted into five peaks centered at 284.4, 284.9, 286.0, 288.1 and 289.0 eV, attributed to CC, CS/CO, CN, CO/CN/CN and COOH species, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) revealed the presence of CN/CN (397.9 eV), CNC (pyridinic-like and pyrrolic-like, 399.9 eV) and C3 N (graphite-like, 401.2 eV) groups [42,43]. The S2p spectrum (Fig. 3d) confirms three main peaks at 162.6, 163.7 and 164.3 eV sequentially. The first two peaks are assigned to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sites of CS covalent bond of the thiophene-like S [23,44], and the last one is attributed to CS specy [45]. The data above are consistent with the FTIR results, revealing that S and N elements have been doped successfully, and there are many hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of S, N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical Properties of the S, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 , UV–Vis absorption spectrum (blue) of aqueous CCA+GITC dots solution exhibits a characteristic absorption peak at 216 nm, which is mainly derived from the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [26]. Additionally, a weak absorption peak of ca. 340 nm in the high background within 300–400 nm can be ascribable to the n-π* transition related to both doping and surface functionalization at the edge of carbon core [46]. Under the illumination of UV (365 nm), strong blue luminescence of the CCA+GITC dots aqueous solution can be clearly observed in the inset of Fig. 4. It is worth noting that there are two peaks existed at 297 and 395 nm for the excitation spectrum of CCA+GITC dots aqueous solution, and none of them can be corresponded well to the absorption peak at ca. 340 nm, suggesting to possess an elusive emission center in the CCA+GITC dots aqueous solution [19]. The maximum emission wavelength of CCA+GITC dots aqueous solution is 442 nm corresponding to excitation wavelength of 395 nm.

The PL properties were analyzed to explore the optical properties of as-prepared S, N-CDs. Emission spectra of the CCA+GITC dots aqueous solution were acquired at different excitation wavelengths (λex) with 5 nm increments from 275 to 520 nm as shown in Fig. 5 . When increasing the excitation wavelength from 275 to 385 nm, the emission maxima of CCA+GITC dots maintains at the same position (Fig. 5a), and the emission intensity changes in accord with the variation trend of the excitation intensity in this range (Fig. S2a). The organic fluorophores in CA-derived CDs, which show no excitation dependent emission due to the constant ground state-excitation state energy level difference in the double bond containing functionalities such as carbonyl, amide and imine groups, are expected to contribute to the claimed “excitation independence” [12,46]. Further increasing the λex from 390 (nearly excitation maxima) to 415 nm, the fluorescence peak generates obvious red shift from 454 to 482 nm (Fig. 5b), and the emission intensity sharply decreases with the same trend as the excitation spectrum at the range of 390–415 nm (Fig. S2b). This behavior of reduced intensity and red-shift of emission is often associated with the optical selection of different surface- or defect-states in the amorphous carbon shell of CDs [46]. In view of the different energy levels related to different “surface states” [47], the narrow range of excitation-dependent emission observed over 390–415 nm suggests a relatively uniform and well-passivated surface of the CDs. Thus, it is proposed that some small fluorophore molecules exist in our CA-derived CDs solution and bond on the surface of the CDs after carbonization, which is also proved by dialysis treatment of the CDs aqueous solution against deionized water using the dialysis bags with molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da (Fig. S3). Simultaneously, the emission wavelength (λem) of CCA+GITC dots maintains again almost unvaried at ca. 480 nm, when excited within λex range of 420–450 nm (Fig. 5c). The corresponding emission intensity continues to decrease in this range, yet the trend to slow down is changed suddenly at λex = 420 nm (Fig. S2c, d). Further excited with the excitation wavelength up to 520 nm, the emission wavelength shows again remarkable red shift phenomenon with the maximized emission wavelength at ca. 570 nm although the emission intensity sharply decreases even nearly disappears at the last excitations as exhibited in Fig. 5d.

Additionally, the PL spectra of CCA+GITC dots in aqueous solution are also illustrated fully in 3D-measurements (Fig. 6a), which appears two emission center of λem = 450 and 480 nm corresponding to the excitation wavelength of λex = 295 and 395 nm respectively. As mentioned above, it should be noted that there is a clear bump (indicated by a black arrow) appeared at the emission wavelength of ca. 480 nm with the excitation wavelength up to 420 nm. Fig. 6b shows the quantitative changes of emission wavelengths with the excitation wavelengths in the range of 270–520 nm. Obviously, a boundary exists naturally at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm and emission wavelength dramatically red shifts after keeping stable with the excitation wavelength up to 450 nm, which makes it glow yellow light when exposed to a laser (the right inset of Fig. 6b). To our knowledge, it is first time to observe such phenomenon in this type of CDs, and it is also impossible for the CDs solution to only possess a kind of structure. One possibility is that (NH2)2 CNH and HSCN groups respectively undergo a chemical reaction with CA in the synthesis of CDs due to the breaking of hydrogen bond, which inevitably results in forming two carbogenic cores with inconsistent surface states each other after carbonization. Therefore, one reasonable explanation for our observations is the possible co-existence of two types of CDs (labeled as N-CDs and S-CDs in the inset of Fig. 6b), and only one of them maintains fluorescent with the excitation wavelength up to 420 nm, which should be S-doped CDs because N-doping CDs often possess more outstanding fluorescence properties compared to those of S-doping under the same condition.

To further understand the origin of the CDs fluorescence, the Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique was utilized to probe the fluorescence lifetime of CCA+GITC dots with different synthesis condition (time, temperature and molar ratio), the results are exhibited and compared in Fig. 7 and Table 1 , respectively. The fluorescence decay curves of all CDs samples can be fitted using triexponential function I(t) based on a nonlinear least squares analysis using the following equation:
(1)
I
t
=
A 1
exp
−
t
τ 1
+
A 2
exp
−
t
τ 2
+
A 3
exp
−
t
τ 3
where τ1, τ2 and τ3 represent the decay time constants, A1, A2 and A3 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ1, τ2 and τ3 respectively, which is scarce because most of CDs now are the biexponential feature for fluorescence lifetime [48,49]. The average lifetime (τavg) of CDs was calculated using equation:
(2)
τ avg
=
A 1
τ 1
2
+
A 2
τ 2
2
+
A 3
τ 3
2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
+
A 3
τ 3

The decay fitting results are listed in Table 1, with two short life of τ1 and τ2 and a long lifetime τ3 for each sample. Generally, the biexponential behavior of lifetime can imply two different emissive sites (fast and slow decay components), which correspond to the recombination of surface state and molecular state respectively [26,49]. Compared to surface/defect state, the emission originating from molecular state generally exhibits a longer recombination lifetime [26]. Thus, the short lifetimes (τ1 and τ2) may be attributed to surface state, while the long lifetime (τ3) may be corresponded to molecular state. This coincides with the proposal that two kinds of CDs coexist in the solution due to the chemical participations of different groups, and the surface states bonded on these CDs are certainly corresponded to the τ1 and τ2 here.

As exhibited in Table 1, τ1 and τ2 as well as the proportions (A1 and A2) achieve maximum at the molar ratio of GITC/CA = 42:5, and the QY is sharply increased to 17.5% compared to that of GITC/CA = 25:5, suggesting the lifetime change is mainly the result of the changing surface state of CDs with the increasing molar ratio of GITC/CA. With the increase of reaction temperatures at the molar ratio of GITC/CA = 42:5 for 2 h, τ1 and τ2 have got maximum at 200 °C with the proportions continued to slightly increase at 240 °C, yet τavg is top at 200 °C, which indicates high temperature (>200 °C) would affect surface state of CDs to weaken their lifetime. In the bottom of Table 1, it seems that τ1 and τ2 as well as the proportions reach optimal time of 2 h at the molar ratio of GITC/CA = 42:5 for 200 °C, and longer time would be disadvantageous for the surface states bonded on CDs. Additionally, the changes of τ3 as well as the proportion are very easy to understand in view of the relation to molecular state, which sufficiently illustrates that the carbonaceous core is at the cost of consuming molecular fluorophores formed at first [12,50,51]. From the above, appropriate synthesis conditions play a key role to form the surface state and molecular state, which will finally determine the PL properties of as-prepared CDs. On the other hand, τ1 and τ2 are not synchronized to undergo changes with increasing reaction conditions although their proportions indeed increased gradually, which largely causes the changes of average lifetime as shown in Table 1. Therefore, this similarly support the propose that different structures (induced by different groups of (NH2)2 CNH and HSCN in this work) of S, N-dopants can significantly affect the PL properties of as-prepared CDs [26].

Up to now, various methods have been developed to prepare suitable fluorescent materials to detect metal ions of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+, etc. [52–58] To explore the fluorescence selectivity of the CDs towards Ag+ in this work, the fluorescent characteristics in the presence of different environmentally-relevant metal ions, including Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Ba2+, Na+, Li+, Al3+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Ag+ at the concentration of 250 μM, were studied as shown in Fig. 8 . The fluorescence intensity of the S, N-CDs was quenched the strongest in the presence of Ag+, yet a lower fluorescence quenching effect was found in the presence of other metal ions, suggesting that the CDs can be used effectively to monitor Ag+ in an aqueous solution. To date, two kinds of viewpoints can be found in the high selectivity of the CDs towards Ag+: I. interaction between Ag+ and the carboxylate or hydroxyl groups bonded on the surface of CDs, which makes the CDs accelerate the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron-transfer process, and results in a substantial decrease in the fluorescence of CDs [32]; II. the strong binding affinity and rapid chelating kinetics between Ag+ and the functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups) on the surface of CDs, which allows a change in the electronic structure of CDs to facilitate the non-radiative recombination through an effective electron transfer process finally [33,34].

The concentration dependent fluorescence measurements of the CDs at different concentrations of Ag+ were monitored to investigate the quenching mechanism of Ag+. As shown in Fig. 9a, fluorescence spectra of the CDs solution with the adding Ag+ undergo a clear red-shift except for weakened intensity compared to that of the original CDs solution, which is scarce in a quenching process of CDs. Interestingly, the emission position stay at ca. 470 nm along with the end of red-shift, which is nearly coincided with the emission wavelength of the S-CDs appeared at λex = 420 nm. Thus, it is proposed that the fluorescence quenching is induced mainly by the interaction between N-CDs and Ag+ at first. With increasing concentrations of Ag+ at 1.0, 10.0 and 100 μM, PL intensities nearly maintain stable rather than weakened gradually, which suggests that there should be some other fluorophores to fill the gap of fluorescence resulted from the quenching of N-CDs towards Ag+. It is noticed that S-CDs are already starting to react on Ag+, hence, SCN group existed on the surface of S-CDs would be the most likely due to active S element, which may be ascribed to the metal-enhanced fluorescence mechanism [29,36]. Continued to adding Ag+ along with the concentration of Ag+ increased to 0.3 mM, emission intensity of the CDs solution decreases dramatically, suggesting high sensitivity of the CDs towards Ag+ due to the combined effect of both CDs. Even at higher concentrations of Ag+, there is a gradual reduction in the emission intensities of the CDs solution, yet it seems to slow down since the concentration of Ag+ up to 1.0 mM.

Generally, the fluorescence quenching mechanism can be divided into static and dynamic quenching. The static quenching is due to the formation of the ground state complex of fluorescence substance and the quencher, and the interaction between them does not change the chemical structure and activity of fluorophores. Whereas the interaction between the fluorescence molecules in excited states and quencher will result in dynamic quenching, which creates a non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent ground state relied on the diffusional collision each other [59]. To get further insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism by Ag+, the emission intensity of the CDs-Ag+ system was analyzed according to Stern-Volmer equation (dynamic quenching, Eq. (3)) and Lineweaver–Burk equation (static quenching, Eq. (4)), respectively [60,61]:
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
C q
= 1 +
K sv
C q
(4)
1
F 0
− F
=
1
F 0
+
K LB
F 0
C q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Ag+ respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, kq is the bimolecular quenching rate constant and τ0 is the lifetime of the fluorophore in the absence of quencher, Cq is the concentration of quencher, KLB is the static quenching constant. As shown in Fig. 9b, the F0/F ratio slows down at the concentration of Ag+ up to 1.0 mM, revealing the different quenching mechanism existed before and after this concentration. According to Eq. (3), the ratio F0/F versus CAg + less than 1.0 mM was plotted in Fig. 9b (red line). The regression equation was F0/F = 0.8316(±0.4042) + 12.5571(±0.7884) CAg +, R2 = 0.9768. Based on the fluorescence lifetime of 7.71 × 10−9 s, the kq was calculated to be 1.63 × 1012 L mol−1 s−1. This value is obviously higher over the limiting diffusion constant Kdif of the biomolecule (Kdif = 2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1) [62], suggesting that the fluorescence quenching was induced by specific interaction between S-CDs and Ag+. Additionally, static quenching according to Eq. (4) is also attempted to analyze this data, yet it is very unbefitting as exhibited in Fig. S4. However, the inset of Fig. 9b shows that the fluorescence quenching of CDs solution by Ag+ fits well with the Lineweaver–Burk equation in the concentrations more than 1.0 mM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9689. Hence, the interaction of CDs and Ag+ is possibly static quenching in this range, and KLB was found to be 94.5 M. The moderate static quenching constant of CDs to Ag+ suggests the formation of static CDs-Ag+ complex, the energy transfer between them finally causes the fluorescence quenching of CDs.

Now that quenching process of CDs towards Ag+ is such complex, FTIR spectrum and fluorescence lifetime of the CDs solution added Ag+ (1.0 mM) were collected to further understand quenching root of the CDs-Ag+ systems. As shown in Fig. 10a, vibrational peaks of the OH and NH in the range of 3250–3000 cm−1 [26,38,39] are weakened upon the addition of Ag+, and the peak related to CN group thoroughly disappears and the CN and CS absorption peaks are enhanced to merge together, suggesting that most functional groups distributed in the surface of CDs would be affected by the added Ag+ [32–34] and SCN group bonded on the surface of S-CDs possesses more strong binding affinity with Ag+ to result in dynamic quenching via an effective electron transfer process. Because it seems that S atom is not in two places at once in view of the possible structure of S-CDs, it is not clear whether there is a fluorescence quenching contribution from the CN group or not. Fig. 10b shows the changes of fluorescence lifetime after the addition of Ag+, the surface state corresponding to τ1 has disappeared; τ2 and τ3 are also weakened. It's worth mentioning that considerable sediments were separated from the CDs solution along with the Ag+ concentrations up to 1.0 Mm, which means that most CDs, especially S-CDs, would be pulled out of the solution by Ag+ via the CDs-Ag+ complex generated in static quenching process [59]. Obviously, this process is a promising method for the fabrication of photoluminescent S, N-CDs to monitor Ag+ once fluorescent Ag+ platform would be built up using the CDs in this work.

In summary, a simple and low-cost strategy for one-step synthesis of luminescent S, N-CDs was developed based on a hydrothermal method by using guanidine thiocyanate as S and N elements dopant. The as-prepared CDs solution emitted deep blue color fluorescence and the highest QY is 23%, and the PL spectra showed a repetitive emission process from excitation-independent to excitation-dependent, which was proposed to be co-existence of two types of CDs with different surface states, and this was further proved by the triexponential feature of fluorescence lifetimes of CDs. Furthermore, the as-prepared CDs can be used as a sensing probe for the detection of Ag+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. More importantly, the changes of fluorescence intensity exhibited both dynamic and static quenching in the different concentration ranges of Ag+, which should be resulted from the multiple surface states of two types of CDs. Especially for the SCN group, it was first time to find the interaction with Ag+ to quench. This strategy may offer a new approach for the development of inexpensive and sensitive sensors in the application of industrial pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work in China was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51502189, 11747063, 11704274, 11705124, and 11602157), and the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (No. 2017163).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.10.004.
